skill.name: azure-resource-manager-sql-dotnet
skill.description: Guidance for using Azure.ResourceManager.Sql in .NET to manage Azure SQL servers, databases, elastic pools, firewall rules, and related management-plane resources.
skill.summary: Manage Azure SQL resources with the Azure Resource Manager SDK for .NET.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical risk from code execution, network, and credential patterns, but review found these are Markdown examples, not executable skill code. The confirmed risks are insecure Azure SQL sample defaults, including a hardcoded administrator password, public network access, and broad firewall examples. Publish with a warning that examples must be hardened before production use.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded SQL Administrator Credential in Example
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The server creation example uses literal administrator credentials. Users could copy this into deployed infrastructure and create predictable secret handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The C# sample explicitly sets administrator login values and a literal password. It is example code, so malicious intent is not confirmed, but production copy risk is clear.

medium_findings.title: Broad Public Network and Firewall Defaults
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The example enables public network access and shows broad firewall rules. These patterns can expose Azure SQL resources if reused without restriction.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sample sets public network access and includes firewall rule examples with broad addresses. These are legitimate ARM examples, but they require hardening before use.

low_findings.title: Credential Environment Variable Documentation
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The skill documents service principal credential variables, including a client secret. This is standard Azure guidance, but secret storage warnings should be stronger.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential variables are presented as configuration examples, with no exfiltration or code execution. The concern is operational handling of secrets.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The many Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown fences and inline code examples. No executable Ruby code or dynamic shell invocation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious backticks occur in Markdown code fences, tables, and inline identifiers. The file is documentation and contains no script that executes those strings.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography alerts are text matches in front matter or table headings, not cryptographic API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review found no hashing, encryption, or cipher implementation. The matched lines are documentation headings and SKU descriptions.

low_findings.title: Request Error Message Logging
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The error handling sample prints request exception messages. This is common debugging practice, but production logs should avoid exposing sensitive request details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sample logs Azure request failure details but does not perform reconnaissance. The residual risk is possible information disclosure in copied production logging.

dangerous_patterns.title: Literal Password in Provisioning Sample
dangerous_patterns.description: The provisioning sample includes a literal SQL administrator password value. Replace this with a secure secret source before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The password value is visibly hardcoded inside a resource creation object. This is a concrete insecure example pattern, even though it is not malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Public Azure SQL Exposure Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample combines public network access with broad firewall examples. Restrict access to private networking or approved client ranges.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines show public exposure and firewall values in the same server provisioning workflow. The risk is clear if users copy the defaults.

content.value_statement: Provisioning Azure SQL resources with the ARM SDK can be difficult to map correctly. This skill gives focused .NET examples for servers, databases, pools, firewall rules, and failover groups.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Azure.ResourceManager.Sql instead of Microsoft.Data.SqlClient.
Shows package installation and DefaultAzureCredential setup for .NET projects.
Provides C# examples for creating Azure SQL servers, databases, and elastic pools.
Demonstrates firewall rule configuration and Azure SQL resource listing patterns.
Lists key resource types and common database and elastic pool SKUs.
limitations: It does not execute SQL queries or perform data-plane operations.
It does not deploy resources by itself; users must run and adapt the examples.
Examples include placeholder names, IP ranges, and credentials that need replacement.
Linked reference files may not be included in this package.

use_cases.title: Provision Azure SQL Infrastructure
use_cases.description: Create repeatable .NET workflows for Azure SQL servers, databases, firewall rules, and elastic pools.

use_cases.title: Add Management Features to an App
use_cases.description: Use ARM SDK patterns to manage database resources from internal tools or platform services.

use_cases.title: Standardize Cloud Database Patterns
use_cases.description: Review supported resource types, SKU choices, and management-plane boundaries before creating team templates.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Starter Server Example
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for Azure SQL management-plane code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create an Azure SQL server with Azure.ResourceManager.Sql in .NET. Explain each required object and where credentials come from.

prompt_templates.title: Add Database and Firewall Management
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate provisioning workflow for a server and database.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a .NET workflow that creates an Azure SQL database and adds a restricted firewall rule for a known client IP range.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Elastic Pool Automation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced resource management for multiple databases.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Azure.ResourceManager.Sql plan for managing an elastic pool and moving databases into it. Include scaling and error handling considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure SQL ARM provisioning approach for production readiness. Focus on identity, secret handling, network exposure, idempotency, and failure handling.

output_examples.input: User asks how to create an Azure SQL server with the ARM SDK.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines the management-plane setup, required package references, credential approach, resource group lookup, and server creation flow.

output_examples.input: User asks whether this skill can run SQL queries.
output_examples.output: The skill explains that query execution is data-plane work and directs the user to Microsoft.Data.SqlClient instead.

output_examples.input: User asks for an elastic pool management plan.
output: Choose the pool SKU and capacity.
Create or update the pool through the server resource.
Move databases into the pool and handle long-running operations.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential and least-privilege Azure roles for management operations.
Replace sample passwords, names, and IP ranges before using any provisioning example.
Prefer private networking and restricted firewall rules for production Azure SQL resources.
anti_patterns: Do not use this management-plane SDK to execute SQL statements or stored procedures.
Do not copy sample administrator passwords into deployed environments.
Do not enable broad public access without a documented network requirement.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps write .NET code that manages Azure SQL resources through Azure Resource Manager.

faq.question: Can it run SQL queries?
faq.answer: No. It is for management-plane operations. Use Microsoft.Data.SqlClient for queries and connections.

faq.question: Which authentication approach does it show?
faq.answer: It uses DefaultAzureCredential and references Azure environment variables for subscription and service principal configuration.

faq.question: Does it create production-ready code automatically?
faq.answer: No. The examples need secure credentials, restricted networking, naming rules, and environment-specific settings.

faq.question: What Azure SQL resources are covered?
faq.answer: It covers servers, databases, elastic pools, firewall rules, failover groups, and related ARM resource types.

faq.question: What should I review before using the examples?
faq.answer: Review identity permissions, secret handling, network exposure, SKU cost, region choice, and long-running operation behavior.
